lesson.title: Functions II: inside the function
masteryOutcomes: Understand the structure of a function, including its name, parameters, indentation, and return value.
Call functions with arguments correctly and trace how values are passed from the call site to parameters, verifying the flow using print statements.

concept: How values flow from a function call into parameters, through the indented body, and back out via return
prerequisites: Defining a simple function with def and calling it

explanations.text: A function is like a little machine with an input slot and an output slot. When you call greet("Ana"), the value "Ana" slides into the input slot (the parameter), the machine does its indented steps, and return pushes the result back out to whoever asked. Adding print() inside is like putting a window on the machine so you can watch the value move through.

A function has four parts: a name (after def), parameters in parentheses (placeholders for incoming values), an indented body (the steps that run), and an optional return (the value sent back to the call site). When you call double(5), Python copies 5 into the parameter n, runs the body, and replaces the call double(5) with whatever return gives back. Try it in the editor: def double(n): return n * 2, then print(double(5)) shows 10. Printing the parameter inside the body — print("n is", n) — lets you verify exactly what value arrived.

At the call site, Python evaluates each argument first, then binds those values to the parameter names in order — this is positional matching, so add(2, 10) makes a=2 and b=10. The body runs with those bindings in its own local scope; names inside the function are separate from names outside. When Python hits return, the function ends immediately and the return value becomes the value of the call expression, so result = add(2, 10) stores 12. A function with no return gives back None. print inside the body writes to the screen but sends nothing back — that is why print and return are different jobs, and why tracing with prints lets you watch the flow without changing it.

examples:
def double(n):
    print("n received:", n)
    return n * 2

result = double(5)
print("result:", result)

def greet(name):
    return "Hello, " + name + "!"

message = greet("Ana")
print(message)

hintLadder.text: Start with the skeleton: def, the function name, parentheses with a parameter name inside, then a colon.
Everything the function does must be indented under the def line — check that your body lines start with four spaces.
The function computes a value, but does the caller ever receive it? What keyword sends a value back out?
Not sure what value the parameter got? Add print("parameter is", <parameter name>) as the first line of the body and run it to watch the value arrive.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes print and return are the same, e.g. 'my function returns the value because it prints it'
misconceptions.correction: print shows a value on the screen; return hands the value back to the caller. Test it: if double uses print instead of return, then result = double(5) followed by print(result) shows None — the caller got nothing back.

misconceptions.trigger: Puts the body at the same indentation as def, or mixes indentation levels
misconceptions.correction: The body must be indented under the def line — that indentation is how Python knows which lines belong to the function. Un-indented lines are outside the function and run immediately.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks the parameter name at the definition must match the variable name at the call, e.g. 'I must call double(n) with a variable named n'
misconceptions.correction: Parameters are just local placeholders. Calling double(x) copies the value of x into n; the names are independent. Print n inside the body to prove it received x's value.

misconceptions.trigger: Swaps argument order and expects the same result, e.g. describe("Ana", 30) vs describe(30, "Ana")
misconceptions.correction: Arguments match parameters by position: the first argument goes to the first parameter. Add print(param1, param2) as the first body line and run both calls to see the values land in different slots.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes code after return in the body and expects it to run
misconceptions.correction: return ends the function immediately — lines after it in the same path never run. Move any print-tracing lines above the return.

misconceptions.trigger: Defines the function but never calls it, then says 'nothing happened'
misconceptions.correction: def only teaches Python the recipe; nothing runs until you call it. Add a call like print(double(5)) below the definition and press Run.

checks.prompt: Write a function add(a, b) that prints "a is" and the value of a, prints "b is" and the value of b, then returns a + b. Call it as print(add(3, 4)).
acceptanceCriteria: Defines add with exactly two parameters using def and a colon
Body lines are indented under the def line
Body prints both parameter values before returning
Uses return a + b (not print) to send the sum back
Running it outputs the two trace lines followed by 7
checks.passFeedback: You just watched 3 and 4 flow into a and b and the sum 7 flow back out — that is the whole life of a function call.
checks.failFeedback: Check three things: the body is indented, you print a and b inside the function, and you use return (not print) for the sum. Then run it again.

checks.prompt: Predict, then verify: if double(n) uses print(n * 2) instead of return n * 2, what does print(double(5)) show?
acceptanceCriteria: States that 10 is printed by the function body
States that None is printed afterwards because the function returns nothing
Verifies the prediction by running the code in the editor
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — print shows a value, return hands it back. A function without return gives back None.
checks.failFeedback: Type the version with print instead of return into the editor and run print(double(5)) — look closely at the second line of output.

practice.prompt: Write a function rectangle_area(width, height) that returns width * height. Add a print statement as the first line of the body that shows both parameter values. Then call it twice — rectangle_area(3, 5) and rectangle_area(5, 3) — printing each result, and watch how the values land in the parameters.
practice.starterCode:
def rectangle_area(width, height):
    # print the parameters here, then return the area
    pass

# call it twice and print the results
acceptanceCriteria: First body line prints both parameter values
Uses return width * height, not print, for the result
Calls the function twice with the arguments in different orders and prints both results
Runs without errors and both calls print 15

practice.prompt: In your own words, trace what happens step by step when Python runs result = add(2, 10) for def add(a, b): return a + b.
rubric: Says the arguments 2 and 10 are matched to parameters a and b by position
Says the indented body runs with those values
Says return sends 12 back to the call site
Says result stores the returned value 12

practice.prompt:
Given def half(n): return n / 2, what does this code print?

x = half(8)
print(x)
options: Nothing — half only computes, it never prints
4.0, because return sends the value back and x stores it
None, because the function has no print inside
8, because x keeps its original argument
practice.explanation: half(8) binds n to 8, the body returns 4.0, that value replaces the call, and x stores it — so print(x) shows 4.0. return delivering the value is exactly why the outer print works.

pedagogyRules: Always have the student verify data flow by printing the parameter inside the body — do not just assert what the value is; make them see it in the Output panel.
When output is None or missing, first ask whether the function uses return or only print — that distinction is the heart of this lesson.
Name the four parts (name, parameters, indented body, return) consistently in every explanation so the vocabulary sticks.
If the student says they already know functions, respect it: skip re-explanation and ask them to predict the print-vs-return check output; if they nail it, advance.
One idea per turn: never explain parameters, indentation, and return in the same breath — pick the one the student's code shows they need.
If the student jumps ahead (e.g. asks about default arguments or *args), give a one-line answer, then steer back: 'that builds on exactly what we're tracing now.'
When frustration shows, shrink the task: trace a one-parameter function with a single print before returning to the two-parameter exercise.
Keep the student in the editor: ask them to change one thing, press Run, and report what the Output panel shows.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when the actual run output matches the expected trace lines and return value. If the student's add(3, 4) prints None or errors, say what the output shows and point to the return-vs-print or indentation fix — never call it correct.

Read the real interpreter message. IndentationError means the body indentation is wrong; NameError on a parameter means the function was likely called without an argument or the name is misspelled. Name the exact cause from the message; do not invent one.

Give exactly one hint from the ladder, then let the student edit and run before the next hint. If they are still stuck after hint 4, walk through tracing a one-parameter function together instead of revealing the full solution.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip tracing and just get the answer
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Offer a deal: they predict the output of one two-line trace before running it. If the prediction is right, advance immediately; if not, that gap is exactly what the tracing prints will close.

chunks.text:
A Python function has four parts: a name (after the def keyword), parameters in parentheses (placeholders for incoming values), an indented body (the lines that run when the function is called), and an optional return statement (the value sent back to the caller). Example:

def double(n):
    return n * 2

Here the name is double, the parameter is n, the body is the single indented return line, and n * 2 is the return value.

When you call a function, Python matches arguments to parameters by position: in add(2, 10) with def add(a, b), the value 2 goes into a and 10 goes into b. The parameter names are local placeholders — calling double(x) copies the value of x into n; the outer variable name does not need to match the parameter name.

return and print do different jobs. return hands a value back to the call site so it can be stored or reused: result = double(5) makes result equal 10. print only shows a value on the screen and sends nothing back — a function that only prints returns None. return also ends the function immediately; lines after it never run.

You can watch values flow through a function by adding print statements inside the body. Worked example:

def double(n):
    print("n received:", n)
    return n * 2

result = double(5)
print("result:", result)

Running this in the editor prints 'n received: 5' (proving 5 arrived in n) and then 'result: 10' (proving the returned value came back and was stored).

The indented body is how Python knows which lines belong to the function. Every body line must be indented the same amount (four spaces is standard) under the def line. A line at the same level as def is outside the function and runs immediately. Also remember: def only defines the recipe — nothing inside runs until the function is called.

celebration.headline: You can see inside a function now.
celebration.recap: You named all four parts of a function — name, parameters, indented body, return — and used print statements to watch real values flow from the call site into parameters and back out through return.
celebration.unlocked: Next: use those returned values to make decisions with if statements.

summaryBullets: You can now name all four parts of a function — its name, parameters, indented body, and return value — and spot each one in real code.
You can now call a function with arguments and predict exactly which parameter each value lands in.
You can now drop print statements inside a function body to prove what values it received and what it sends back.
You can now explain why return hands a value to the caller while print only shows it — and why a print-only function gives back None.
openingLine: Hey {name} — last time you built functions; today we open the hood and watch values actually travel through them. When you call double(5), where does that 5 go? Let's trace it and find out.
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: You already defined and called functions with parameters — now you'll trace exactly how each argument flows into its parameter and back out through return, using print statements as your window.

dialogueExemplars.title: Print is not return — a confident mix-up

turns.text: My function definitely returns the value — I wrote print(n * 2) inside it and I can see 10 in the output.

I love that you tested it! But print and return do different jobs — print shows a value, return hands it back to the caller. Try this in the editor: result = double(5), then print(result). What does the second line show?
Because your function showed 10 on screen but never sent it back — so result caught nothing, and Python calls nothing None. Swap print(n * 2) for return n * 2 and run it again — what changes?
Now it prints 10 for result. So return is what actually delivers the value.
Exactly — return is the delivery, print is just the window. That distinction will save you hours of debugging later. Ready to trace a two-parameter function next?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I need to trace values at all?

turns.text: Why do I even need to trace how values move? I can just write the function and hope it works.

Fair question! Here's the real-world use: when a payment app charges the wrong amount, an engineer's first move is printing what value each function actually received — hoping doesn't find bugs, tracing does. Want to try it on a tiny example right now?
In the editor, write def add(a, b) with print("a is", a) as the first line, return a + b as the second, then call print(add(3, 4)) and press Run. What does the output tell you about where the 3 went?
It says 'a is 3', so the first argument went into a.
You just did exactly what professionals do to debug — proved where a value landed instead of guessing. Now, without running it: what would 'a is' show if you called add(4, 3) instead?

dialogueExemplars.title: I already know functions — can we skip?

turns.text: I already covered functions last lesson. Can we skip this one?
You did build functions, so let's not re-drill — let's verify with one quick prediction. If double uses print(n * 2) instead of return, what does print(double(5)) output?

Close, and this is the exact trap this lesson exists for: it prints 10, then None, because the function never returned anything. Run it in the editor and see — then we'll make sure return never surprises you again. Deal?
